claims: DSMIL-LocNet, a Multiple Instance Learning framework, can detect and temporally localize whale calls using only bag-level labels.
Longer audio contexts improve classification performance with F1 scores ranging from 0.8 to 0.9.
Medium instance durations ensure localization precision between 0.65 and 0.70.
Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) can enhance scalable marine monitoring by reducing the need for precise temporal annotations.
methods: Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) framework for processing audio recordings as bags of instances.
Dual-stream architecture combining CNN-based spectrogram encoding and MLP-based temporal feature extraction.
Attention-based instance selection for temporal localization.
Hierarchical audio processing to segment recordings into bags and instances.
Use of a 4-component loss function addressing class imbalance, temporal coherence, sparsity, and instance consistency.
limitations: The model's performance is variable across different bag and instance durations, with less predictable patterns in temporal localization.
The approach may face challenges in handling diverse acoustic events in complex marine soundscapes.
paper_analyses.conclusion: DSMIL-LocNet effectively detects and temporally localizes whale calls in long-duration recordings using weakly labeled data, demonstrating the potential of MIL to transform marine acoustic monitoring by balancing broad temporal analysis with precise localization.

claims: Neural networks autoencoder (NN-AE) demonstrated superior performance in anomaly detection with high recall and no false normal classifications.
Traditional models exhibited false normal fractions exceeding 0.9, indicating they failed to detect the majority of true anomalies.
This study is the first effort to develop automated methods using machine learning and deep learning for anomaly detection in acoustic telemetry data.
methods: Data pre-processing, including cleaning, normalizing, and splitting the dataset.
Feature engineering to create new informative features for model training.
Labelling process based on criteria capturing abnormal fish movement patterns.
Resampling strategy to adjust irregular sampling of normal samples.

Training of unsupervised classifiers: Isolation Forest (IF), Local Outlier Factor (LOF), Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (DBSCAN), Neural Network Autoencoder (NN-AE), and Long Short-Term Memory-based autoencoder (LSTM-AE).
limitations: NN-AE faced challenges in accurately learning normal movement patterns when these patterns gradually deviated from anomalous ones.
Traditional models were prone to high false normal rates, predicting anomalous as normal.
LSTM-AE quickly overfitted the data, even after resampling efforts.
paper_analyses.conclusion: The study successfully developed and evaluated machine learning and deep learning models for anomaly detection in large-scale acoustic telemetry data, with NN-AE showing superior performance in identifying anomalies without false normal classifications. This highlights its potential for ensuring data integrity in telemetry studies, although challenges remain in accurately learning gradually deviating normal patterns.

claims: The proposed semi-supervised acoustic bird detector can detect time-overlapping calls when separated in frequency and requires fewer labeled training samples.
The classifier achieves a mean F0.5 score of 0.701 across 315 classes from 110 bird species with an average of 11 labeled training samples per class.
The detector outperforms the state-of-the-art BirdNET classifier on a test set of 103 bird species despite using significantly fewer labeled training samples.
The method demonstrates high precision in environments with minimal labeled training data, even in rich soundscapes like Singapore.
methods: Segmentation using an energy-based technique to isolate individual bird calls.
Data compression through a convolutional deep auto-encoder to learn a compressed representation of the segments.
Contrastive representation learning to ensure translational invariance and similarity in embeddings for similar sounds.
Supervised refinement and classification using a classifier trained on embeddings from the contrastive learning stage.
limitations: The rich soundscape in Singapore presents challenges in suppressing false positives on raw, continuous data streams.
The method may not capture entire call sequences, as single calls or songs can be fragmented into multiple TFRs.
paper_analyses.conclusion: The paper presents a semi-supervised bird vocalization classifier that effectively detects time-overlapping calls with minimal labeled data, outperforming existing methods like BirdNET in certain conditions and demonstrating potential for high-precision monitoring in complex soundscapes.

claims: acoupi is an open-source Python framework that simplifies the creation and deployment of smart bioacoustic devices.
acoupi integrates audio recording, AI-based data processing, data management, and real-time wireless messaging into a unified framework.
The framework allows users to customize, extend, or select specific components to fit unique monitoring needs.
acoupi can be deployed on low-cost hardware like the Raspberry Pi and can be customized for various applications.
methods: Integration of two bioacoustic classifiers: BirdNET for bird species and BatDetect2 for UK bat species.
Deployment of acoupi-powered devices in a UK urban park for a month-long test.
Use of a modular approach to configure the entire bioacoustic workflow from data collection to wireless transfer.
Testing of acoupi on Raspberry Pi 4 Model B with Linux OS.
limitations: The need for more extensive field testing under varying network connectivity and power availability.
The current version is limited to audio recordings and relies on Wi-Fi for communication.
Integration of additional AI-based models is necessary to accommodate a greater diversity of species and applications.
Potential processing backlogs and system overloads if processing times exceed recording intervals.
paper_analyses.conclusion: acoupi provides a standardized and simplified framework for deploying AI-powered passive acoustic monitoring systems, facilitating their adoption by researchers and conservationists. Despite successful initial testing, further field evaluations and model integrations are necessary to enhance its applicability and reliability.

claims: The NEHD-based approach offers a lightweight and efficient solution for underwater target recognition, significantly reducing computational costs while maintaining accuracy.
NEHD matches the performance of large pre-trained models with greater efficiency.
Combining structural and statistical texture features improves classification performance.
NEHD can be used as a feature extractor to enhance model performance with minimal computational overhead.
methods: Adaptation of neural edge histogram descriptors (NEHD) for passive sonar signal classification.
Extraction of structural texture features using edge descriptors.
Extraction of statistical texture features using a histogram layer.
Evaluation of NEHD against pre-trained models like ResNet-50, ViT, PANN, and AST.
Use of the DeepShip dataset for training and testing.
Hyperparameter search for optimal spectrogram features.
limitations: The study acknowledges that while NEHD achieves comparable performance to large models, it may not surpass the highest accuracy achieved by models like PANN.
paper_analyses.conclusion: The study demonstrates that NEHD provides a computationally efficient method for underwater acoustic target recognition, achieving performance comparable to larger models while using significantly fewer parameters. It effectively combines structural and statistical texture features and can enhance model performance when used as a feature extractor.
